content: {'summary': 'The President of the United States serves as head of state and head of government under Article II of the Constitution, wielding executive power with specific enumerated authorities and implied powers.', 'key_points': ["Article II, Section 1 vests 'executive Power' in the President without exhaustive enumeration", 'Constitutional powers include Commander-in-Chief authority, treaty-making (with Senate consent), appointments (with Senate advice and consent), veto power, and pardon power', 'Executive orders derive from constitutional executive power and statutory delegations, subject to judicial review', 'War powers are shared: Congress declares war and funds military, President commands armed forces', 'The President executes laws passed by Congress but cannot unilaterally legislate', 'Term limits (22nd Amendment) restrict service to two elected terms or maximum 10 years'], 'sources': ['U.S. Constitution, Article II', "Congressional Research Service, 'Powers of the President' (RL34507)", 'Constitution Annotated (congress.gov)', 'Federalist Papers No. 69-77', 'Youngstown Sheet & Tube v. Sawyer (1952)'], 'related_fragments': ['frag_gov_gov_separationofpowers_definitional_004', 'frag_gov_gov_vetopower_definitional_007', 'frag_gov_gov_executiveprivilege_definitional_010'], 'last_updated': '2024-01-15', 'credibility_score': 0.98, 'philosophy_guard_passed': True, 'philosophy_guard_note': 'Descriptive claim about presidential systems; no normative advocacy regarding what should be'}
